query: Turkey heatwave June 2026 temperature forecast wildfire risk
answer:
# Turkey Heatwave, Temperature Forecast & Wildfire Risk — June 2026

## Research Report

### 1. Current Temperature Conditions (as of June 16, 2026)

Turkey has experienced a **notably warm 2026**, particularly during winter, with a significant warming pulse forecast for late June.

**Year-to-date temperature anomalies (vs. 1991–2020 normals):**

| City | Region | Early June 2026 Avg Max | Normal | Anomaly |
|------|--------|------------------------|--------|---------|
| **Istanbul** | NW / Marmara | 28.4°C | 24.9°C | **+3.5°C** |
| Antalya | Med. coast | 30.1°C | 30.8°C | −0.7°C |
| Adana | SE Med. | 30.3°C | 31.0°C | −0.7°C |
| Ankara | Central Anatolia | 26.4°C | 26.8°C | −0.4°C |

Istanbul is running significantly above normal (+3.5°C in early June), while southern and central cities are near their climate averages. February 2026 was exceptionally warm across Turkey (+2.0 to +3.0°C above normal).

---

### 2. Late June Forecast: Building Heat Pulse (Jun 23–29)

A pronounced warming event is forecast for the final week of June:

| City | Forecast Peak Temp | Date | June Record (1990–2024) |
|------|--------------------|------|-------------------------|
| **Diyarbakir** | **39.6°C** | Jun 27 | 41.3°C (2024) |
| **Antalya** | **38.2°C** | Jun 29 | 43.3°C (2017) |
| **Adana** | **38.0°C** | Jun 29 | 43.1°C (2024) |
| **Istanbul** | **32–33°C** | Jun 24–25 | 35.0°C (2017) |
| **Ankara** | **32–33°C** | Jun 25–26 | 37.0°C (1996) |

The forecast does **not** currently approach all-time June records but represents temperatures 4–7°C above current mid-June values. The situation bears close monitoring, as the main heat season (July–August) has not yet begun.

---

### 3. Precipitation & Drought Status

**Counter-intuitively, 2026 has been an exceptionally wet year for Turkey:**

| City | 2026 Jan–Jun Precip | Normal | % of Normal |
|------|---------------------|--------|-------------|
| **Adana** | 907.6 mm | 392.4 mm | **231%** |
| **Ankara** | 454.6 mm | 267.5 mm | **170%** |
| **Antalya** | 904.1 mm | 592.0 mm | **153%** |
| **Istanbul** | 478.6 mm | 349.5 mm | **137%** |

Turkey entered summer 2026 with a substantial precipitation surplus. However, June is forecast to be almost completely dry, and if July–August follow their typical near-zero rainfall pattern, the surplus will be rapidly drawn down by evaporation and agricultural demand. **Southeastern Turkey (Diyarbakir) shows zero precipitation forecast over the next 15 days combined with 35–40°C heat**, which will rapidly deplete soil moisture.

---

### 4. Wildfire Risk Assessment

#### Risk-Amplifying Factors:
- **Strong El Niño developing**: 98% probability for Aug–Oct 2026 (NOAA/IRI). Peak Niño-3.4 forecast of +2.03°C (OND 2026), with some individual models reaching +3.4°C ("Super El Niño" territory). El Niño is historically associated with hotter, drier summers in the eastern Mediterranean.
- **Record global warmth continuing**: March 2026 was the warmest March on record (US); February 2026 was the 5th warmest globally (Copernicus, +1.49°C above pre-industrial baseline).
- **Long-term warming trend**: Southeastern Turkey (Diyarbakir, Adana) shows June average maximum temperatures rising +3.0–3.4°C over the past three decades.
- **Imminent dry-down**: The transition into the bone-dry summer regime is underway with essentially zero rainfall forecast.

#### Risk-Mitigating Factors:
- **Wet antecedent conditions**: The exceptional 2026 precipitation surplus (137–231% of normal) means vegetation is well-hydrated entering summer. This reduces early-season fire risk.
- **No extreme heatwave (yet)**: Current forecasts show a notable but not record-breaking heat pulse. Temperatures remain 3–5°C below all-time June records.
- **Turkey's post-2021 investments**: After the catastrophic 2021 fire season (~190,000 ha burned), Turkey expanded its fire-fighting aircraft fleet and early warning systems.
- **El Niño still building**: The peak El Niño effect won't arrive until Oct–Dec 2026; summer months may see moderate rather than strong conditions.

#### Historical Context — 2021 Catastrophe:
- ~300 wildfires in approximately 2 weeks (Jul 28–Aug 2021)
- ~190,000 hectares burned (6× annual average of ~10,000–15,000 ha)
- At least 9 deaths; hundreds of villages evacuated
- Worst-hit: Antalya (Manavgat) and Muğla (Marmaris, Bodrum) provinces
- National all-time temperature record: **49.1°C** at Cizre, Şırnak province (Jul 21, 2021)

#### Net Assessment:
Turkey's wildfire risk for summer 2026 is **elevated relative to the long-term average** due to the developing strong El Niño and continued global warming trend. However, the **wet antecedent conditions substantially mitigate early-season risk**. The critical period will be **late July through August**, when the cumulative drying effect of the rainless summer and the strengthening El Niño converge. The abundant early-year vegetation growth, fueled by above-normal rainfall, could paradoxically increase fire fuel loads later in the season as it dries out.

---

### 5. Key Monitoring Resources

| Source | URL | What It Provides |
|--------|-----|------------------|
| EFFIS (EU Forest Fire Info System) | https://effis.jrc.ec.europa.eu | Real-time fire detection & seasonal forecasts |
| Turkish Met Service (MGM) | https://www.mgm.gov.tr | Official Turkish weather forecasts & warnings |
| Copernicus Climate Service | https://climate.copernicus.eu | Climate anomalies & seasonal outlooks |
| NOAA ENSO Diagnostic | https://www.cpc.ncep.noaa.gov/products/analysis_monitoring/enso_advisory/ensodisc.shtml | El Niño status updates |
| IRI ENSO Forecast | https://iri.columbia.edu/our-expertise/climate/forecasts/enso/current/ | Multi-model ENSO forecast |

---

### 6. Notable: COP31 in Antalya

The **COP31 Climate Conference is scheduled for November 2026 in Antalya, Turkey** — situated in the heart of Turkey's most wildfire-prone Mediterranean coast region and one of the areas hardest hit during the 2021 fires.

---

**Data sources:** Open-Meteo API (ECMWF IFS forecasts, ERA5 reanalysis 1991–2020), NOAA/IRI ENSO diagnostics, Copernicus Climate Change Service, European Forest Fire Information System (EFFIS), RioTimesOnline climate analysis. All data retrieved June 16, 2026.
